title: Integrated transcriptomic and metabolomic analysis reveals the role of CsCLCN7 in regulating calcium-magnesium ratio homeostasis in the clam Cyclina sinensis.
author_string: Ni, Qian, Zhao, Jie, Liu, Qingyuan, Jiang, Kunyin, Ge, Hongxing et al.
abstract: Compared with natural seawater, the concentration and proportion of calcium and magnesium in saline-alkali water are greatly different, which may be a key factor affecting the growth and survival of aquatic animals. In this study, the growth and survival of Cyclina sinensis under different calcium and magnesium ratios (1:1, 1:1.5, 1:2, 1:2.5, 1:3, 1:3.5, 1:4, 1:4.5, 1:5) and low salinity (10 ‰) conditions (60 days) were evaluated. The results showed that low salinity stress and different calcium-magnesium ratios influenced the growth and survival of C. sinensis. Whereas, there was no significant difference in the growth and survival of the clams between the control group and the group in the low salinity (10 ‰) conditions with a ratio of calcium and magnesium ions (1:2.5, 1:3, 1:3.5) close to the normal seawater (1:3). The response mechanism of C. sinensis under different calcium and magnesium ratios (1:1, 1:3, and 1:5 for 72 h) was analyzed through transcriptome and metabolome analysis. A total of 354 differentially expressed genes (DEGs) and 878 differentially expressed metabolites (DEMs) were screened out. The integrated analysis of transcriptome and metabolome showed that the Carbon metabolic pathway and Calcium signaling pathway play important roles in osmoregulation of C. sinensis. Chloride voltage-gated channel 7 (CsCLCN7) was significantly enriched by KEGG and integrated transcriptomic and metabolomic analysis. The product of CLCN7 belongs to the CLC chloride channel protein family and plays an important role in the plasma membrane and intracellular organelles. The full length of CsCLCN7 is 1286 bp with an 819 bp open reading frame (ORF), encoding a chloride channel protein of 272 amino acids with a deduced molecular weight of 30.40 kDa. This result indicates CsCLCN7 may play important roles in regulating calcium‑magnesium ratio homeostasis of the clam C. sinensis. This study provides insights into the osmoregulation of C. sinensis and provides clues for the cultivation of economically important species in saline-alkali environments.
